commentBody:
My five favorite original clues from last week
(in order of appearance):

1. Mickey Mouse  (7)
2. Biggest restaurant size that nevertheless is often topped?  (5)(5)(5)
3. Bar of note?  (4)
4. Current affairs?  (15)
5. They shine during the day  (4)(5)


TRIVIAL
EXTRA LARGE PIZZA
FRET
ELECTRODYNAMICS
SOAP STARS
Super quick but I am in favor of a themed Monday puzzle.

Monday pun day, so good to me.
Thanks, Mamas.
Too many naticks for a Monday. Neal crossed with rama? Cbgb? Fine for a Wednesday, maybe even a Tuesday. But not a Monday. Very punny though! Good theme.

Welp, you had me at CBGB, Ginny and Raji and sealed the deal with GIMLET, though make mine with Vodka, please. I'm even willing to overlook, the misspelled SchMEAR.  :)
Loved your themers with FELINELINE perhaps my fave.  I did get a little kick out of the ....LIST, thinking about someone who really, really wants one.

Off topic, but here goes. Happy Birthday to Mel Brooks, who turned 100 Sunday, June 28 (today or yesterday, depending on when you're reading this). He's been in puzzles going back 40 years or so. Here he is talking about Heddy Lamarr, also no stranger to crosswords. https://youtu.be/ZEQ3OxYL2Co?si=N5gDyRX5mXbAtNSC

[Source for Bacchus's libations]

DIVINEVINE

(I mean grapes from the divinewinevine, of course.)

A great deal of synchronicity for me on our last morning in Scandinavia. We’re here in Stockholm, where we ate the traditional “Swedish dish” (the entry that ended up on the cutting room floor) of meatballs with lingonberry dressing, and where we discussed the NOBELIST LIST (although ultimately deciding not to visit the Nobel Prize center).  Two puzzles for me today, as we’ll be on NY time by tonight.

Okay, a Monday that made me sweat a bit.

The whole CBGB crossing made me very quesy about the upper midwest.  But it was the A in NEAL that had me flyspecking.

Good Monday!

So, the Francis Conjecture almost came into play today, and on a Monday no less. CBGB is a a mystery to me; I guessed GMC but still not sure why they are particularly a big name in pickups and vans more than any other type of automobile; and I had to run the alphabet in my mind for the M in ARMY, since I thought it would be something particular to BTS (which remarkably I did know, but again lots of bands have fans called an ARMY, I think). No shade, because I don’t mind getting a little stumped even on a Monday; alas, this boomer would have gotten ARMY a lot faster if it had been connected to M*A*S*H rather than a Korean boy band. Sigh; that’s the ropes when we get older. 

Before anyone gets upset about too much Hebrew again, I think as spelled LEILA is more Arabic or Persian than Hebrew, but intelligible to either speaker, I think. So unless you have an issue with foreign derivations of any sort in your crosswords you shouldn’t have an issue.

I enjoyed, and this is not a “too hard” complaint, but more evidence that the NYT is willing to add a bit more beef to their early week puzzles even as they may be making their later week puzzles more vegan. I’ll take what I can get. Thanks, Ginny and Rajeswari!
That was bracing for a Monday. I liked it.

Can’t wait to read the hate tomorrow over this one… Not a horrible puzzle, just bad for a Monday. Waaaaaaay too much obscure answers…

Rating:
5/10
What sizzles in bacon is not FAT (57D), but rather water, which boils when it contacts the melted fat, making a sizzling sound.  The hot fat is silent.
I thought the inclusion of ‘ECHO’ fit well with the theming on this puzzle. I enjoyed it!
Enjoyed the wordplay with LEVY/LEVI, but the rhyme beginning with EENIE verges on a NOGO as it has pejorative connotations toward African Americans
Strychnine nine, ermine mine, plane lane...and the list goes on.  Thought the puzzle was fine.  Then again, I am just a rookie around these parts.
That was HARD! I had to resort to google for New York Clubs but given I am two long haul flights away from it I feel I can be forgiven.
Thank you both for a fairly straightforward, fun puzzle! Keep up the masterwork work!

CBGB was a very famous club in NYC back in the day, which of course doesn’t make it any less obscure for a Monday crossword. But NYers of a certain age would consider it a total gimme, while others would find it to me a headscratcher. 

The initials stood for “country, bluegrass and blues,” which was an odd name for what was essentially a punk rock club. 

I guess the original intent went out the window when more money came through the door with punk.

Lovely Monday for me, crunchy but devoid of hiccups. I was completely on the wavelength, but can see where trip-ups might occur. I should write an ode to the legendary CBGB, but I'll spare you and instead just do a shout-out to the M*A*S*H T.V. series intro music: https://youtu.be/GIGYLBVjYZA?is=CSteqfd3zDPorsr7 (I remember when we still watched real-time T.V.. We'd hear the music from another room and come running...) Thank you, Ginny and Rajeswari. This was a great puzzle!

I don't need to say a meowthful to tell you cats are purrfect, but I'm feline like I could.

(In fact, I'm paws-itive.)
"Roue" and "Ione" are nuts for a Monday. Had to resort to the alphabet game when I had the letter where they intersect as my last blank square.

My only look-up was CBGB, to confirm spelling. Otherwise, it was a bit sticky, but Mondays can be pretty mind numbing sometimes, and I appreciated the fun of a real solve.

Pretty snazzy start to the week from a debut duo. I'm looking forward to  a Saturday version.  Thanks, Ginny and Rajeswari.  We'll be waiting.

Sure, it starts with the FELINE LINE.
But then morphs into an aria, soliloquy, or address to the stockholders.

In other news, my PET PEEVE of the moment is that the emus ate my yesterday comment even though I am, like, a genuine native Californian.
NEAL / RAMA and IONE / ROUE got me
Nice puzzle! Fun!

I had no idea there was a theme to this puzzle until I saw the column.   I was doing the puzzle while I was doing other things, and broken up by other activities.   When I was done I just closed it.  I didn't review the clues and answers as I sometimes do.

Just to add to the discussion about 6A CGBD, I was heading for two days in a row without lookups. But I had CGDB. I remembered hearing about the club and it was in the news when it shut down a while ago. Someone in the comments below informed that the name stood for "country, bluegrass and blues." Good to know.

I didn't know if DMC made sense for vehicles.
I had ARMY for the BTS clue, but I didn't know why. Still don't.

So I had to go to Google to look for pickup truck and van manufacturers. There I found GMC. Eureka! Done. Gold star, anyway.

I’m surprised to see so much pushback on CBGB, which for me was a true gimme. Which just goes to show that everyone has different knowledge bases. And why I sometimes have a hard time with a so-called “easy” puzzle.

But overall, I don’t know why anyone would have a problem with this one. A themed Monday; what’s not to love? I thought it was an excellent recreation creation.

About 23D [A quarter or more of one's life, typically] SLEEP...

Whoa. If you sleep a quarter of your life, that's like average six hours a day.  Whoa...Who sleeps six hours a day? Not me. When I was young, I could sleep 15 hours at a stretch.

Of course, it's the perennial topic, that amount of sleep is totally an individual thing. But still, those in the know usually recommend about seven to eight hours a night.

For me, I'm off to dreamland right now. But wait. It's 12:07 am. I just have to do the Wordle...And I wish I had a cat to put out, but I don't.

Really? People think this was too hard?

It did take me almost to my average, but don't we come for the challenge?

Can’t remember the last time I did a Monday. Not even sure what compelled me, other than I ‘accidentally’ did a Wednesday last week, and it was clever. 

I found it pretty easy, and surprised to see the comments. But I have no basis for comparison. But it seemed Monday to me. 

Regarding CBGB. Having lived in the ‘god forsaken’ city for part of my life, it was a gimme. Since I went there quite a few times. That withstanding?
I got zero issue when the paper based in NYC throws a bone to its locals. 

Okay, you live in Ohio or Bangalore. And have never seen the thousands of wannabes wearing that CBGB tee. Understood. 
I don’t remember NOLITA from Bowery from LES. But I ain’t mad if it’s in the grid. But sure. I’ll admit it is a bit tough for those solvers starting on their nytxw journey. 

But as I write this, i realize I have no idea how to judge a Monday. But maybe at least, I’ll be spurred on to do the puzzle every day. 

I don’t know if the commenter is still around, but

“I done did this puzz”
Not too tricky this time - just the night club and the author. I think of myself as well-read, but this crossword does throw up authors I've never heard of. Not that I mind.

ROUE crossing IONE on a monday? cmon. 

thankfully i took the right guess on NEAL / RAMA because i almost left it as NEIL / RIMA, otherwise running the alphabet to find that friggin O wouldn’t have worked. 

i’m down to learn a new word, but when it’s an obscure word that looks more like a typo of another word (ROGUE, who could kinda vaguely count as a ‘hedonistic sort’) then it’s particularly frustrating. 
crossing it with a very unusual name i’ve never heard of anyone having …   what gives

Wow, I’m so surprise that this seems to have been a tough Monday for some. When a grid is on your own wavelength I guess you assume it’s because ‘everyone’ knows the answers. I couldn’t fill this in quickly enough; absolutely not bragging, this happens to me so rarely it’s a true red letter day.

I’ve never been to New York but CBGB was a total gimme. I recall it as a legendary breeding ground for punk bands. Isn’t it where Blondie et al got their starts?

ROUE/IONE also felt straightforward. The first isn’t an everyday word sure, but I suppose as a BA Lit grad I’ve read enough classic literature for it to seem a normal phrase. The second I know only from the crossword, though I have a habit of spelling it like the island, with a final A. 

Well, I reckon I should buy a lottery ticket, this clearly is my lucky day. Though I expect the double poo pick I’m about to start in the Alpacas fields will drop me back down to Earth. Nothing quite like the smell of ammonia as they put a fresh deposit down right where you’ve just cleared to remind you of your true place in the cosmos.

Lovely Monday for me, crunchy but devoid of hiccups. I was completely on the wavelength, but can see where trip-ups might occur. I should write an ode to the legendary CBGB, but I'll spare you and instead just do a shout-out to the M*A*S*H T.V. series intro music: https://youtu.be/GIGYLBVjYZA?is=CSteqfd3zDPorsr7 (I remember when we still watched real-time T.V.. We'd hear the music from another room and come running...) Thank you, Ginny and Rajeswari. This was a great puzzle!

@MOL

Still half-asleep,
but just reread your comment 
to my comment yesterday
about painting our fence.

(And then Huck FINN was in the puzz!)

I responded, but had not read
carefully, so I missed the joke!

Technically, hubby was the one
using Tom’s trick.

He called it a “painting party”
and I bought it.

But it does look beautiful.
And even better…

it’s done!

I once ended a very long streak because the puzzle constructor used the spelling "brier" instead of "briar." (I think the cross word was a proper name, and the spelling looked OK with either an E or an A.) Imagine my horror when I confirmed that "brier" is an acceptable variant. As for the puzzler's motto of "at least I learned something," I did: I learned that, moving forward, I will never spell it "brier" as a matter of principle.
If you'd like a slightly harder Monday, try this one: https://www.newyorker.com/puzzles-and-games-dept/crossword/2026/06/29

Happy Birthday to the 100 year old man, Mel Brooks!!

The duplicated words in the 'theme' helped get me through some of their crossings. 

Creative twists to the clues.

Nice one. Always interested in what winds up on the cutting room floor, which led to the discovery that there is a website of that name focused on video game developers: https://tcrf.net/The_Cutting_Room_Floor This may or may not be lying there: [Handwritten diary] ANALOGLOG

My first time looking up statistics...

"The word "ROUE" (meaning a licentious or dissolute man) has been a popular staple of crossword constructors, appearing in the New York Times crossword 72 times during the modern era (starting from the Will Shortz editing era in late 1993, and over 120 times throughout the entire publication history)."

I figured most solvers would consider it a gimme, but I guess that for a lot of people today was their first encounter.

This was a super-fun Monday! To be honest, I'm usually a little bored by early-week puzzles, so this is high praise from me. The theme was cute and the fill was interesting.

Legitimately shocked so many people don't know CBGB. It was a legendary club back in the day. It's definitely not just a "New York thing," as I first learned about the club while living in a small town in South Dakota.

What fun!  I don't expect either this much challenge or wordplay that's this entertaining on a Monday.  By the time I was past SPARRING RING, I had the theme and wanted to see how many I could get with few or no crosses.  I got INTELLIGENT GENT off just the IN, but I didn't get either NOBELIST LIST or FELINE LINE (adorbs!) without lots of crosses. NOBELIST LIST had me completely baffled: What was that scientist (Linus Pauling) doing in there with all those world peace activists?  So that was a nice surprise.

Also liked the clues for EATEN and PET PEEVES.  I originally had PaTP because I always think that that IONE actress should be IONA.  Well, shouldn't she be?  IONE sounds like a vitamin.

An unusually good Monday.  More like this, please.

My slowest Monday ever. Took me one hour and eight minutes.

I set out to solve the puzzle making use of just the horizontal clues. I failed utterly. After one hour, with just the NE and SW corners filled (and a smattering elsewhere) and no clue as to what the theme was, I abandoned the attempt. Still took me eight minutes to fill in the rest after regrouping.

In retrospect, at least I now understand why I was unable to fully discern any of the thematic entries from their clues. 

Congratulations to those who solved this one using just the horizontals. Perhaps next Monday I'll restrict my attention to just the verticals. Because thematic entries are typically oriented horizontally, that should be a different type of solving experience yet again.

Perhaps not surprisingly, working on a puzzle using only half the clues pretty much destroys one's typical sense of how the puzzle felt. Will be especially interesting to read the comments today.

Nice Monday puzzle. Typical slow start for me, but finally tumbling to the theme was a big turning point and that's always a nice touch. And... puzzle find today - a Sunday from July 24, 2022 by Jessie and Ross Trudeau with the title: "Going Somewhere?" Not easy to describe this one, but a couple of examples: Down answer PENNYLANE with the E at the end crossing the across answer: AERODROM(E) And... down answer RODEODRIV(E) crossing ETHANFROM(E) And... down answer EVERGREENTERRAC(E) crossing IMPOSTERSYNDROM(E) And....down answer PENNSYLVANIAAVENU(E) crossing the 'reveal' ALLROADSLEADTOROM(E) And there were more. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=7/24/2022&h=115a ....

It's easy to see why constructors like words like IONA and IONE ROUE.. and OREO and A-ONE and... For future reference, If it's a New Rochelle (New York) school or a Scottish island, it's IONA; If it's an actress, whose only noteworthy role was almost 40 years ago ("Say Anything", 1989), it's IONE. Apologies to Ms. Skye, but I just checked you out on IMDB, and you know it's true. But, as earworms go, I'm happy to have Peter Gabriel's "In Your Eyes" in my head... https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kU8OJAOMbPg

I'm feeling a little bit sorry for us solvers that we missed out on the 
MEATBALL BALL.

I'm guessing that my invitation got lost in the mail.

If there were super-clever clues, I missed them, because so many segments filled via crossings, without benefit of hints or attention....  
I started with 63A, climbed upward, filled in the NOBELIST LIST and Boom, zipped through the rest of the puzzle....and...Now what am I supposed to do with the rest of my coffee time??

I did have to change SHOO to SCAT...  (we never use "SCAT!"...not sure why that is.)  
Have a good start to your week, everyone!

[London franchise of a NY punky place]

Cute. Fun. 

So ECHO wasn't the revealer?




CBGBGB

They're everywhere when you're looking for them, right?

ESCAPE CAPE
SCAT CAT
FEELER EELER
EATEN TEN
EGRET ET
Never thought I’d give up on a puzzle where “Food is ______ at dinner” was a clue

Crossword word

EPEE (etc.)

Not directly related to the puzzle, which I enjoyed but found crunchier than the usual Monday (not saying it was too hard, just observing that it had some words that I’d expect to see later in the week, e.g. ROUE), BUT:

I happened to be driving in Massachusetts this morning,  Imagine my excitement when I saw an exit for Natick! I had to explain its Crosslandia meaning in to my fellow passengers, who listened politely. And then informed me that it’s apparently pronounced “NAY-tick”, not “NATT-ick”, as I’ve been saying for years. 

The word “endonym” came up in the ensuing conversation. 

Anyway, nerdily exciting for this midwestern Xworder.
Roue and Ione…?!? Not appropriate for a Monday crossword. Those are Tuesday/Wednesday clues.

Sorry, but if you've never heard of CBGB, we can't be friends. The same goes for NEAL Stephenson, although that was a brutally minimalist clue. I'd lend you my copy of "Snow Crash" but Matt never returned it.
Very fun Monday puzzle!

Glad to see CBGB here! In an attempt to provide some evidence that this was truly an iconic place / moment, here are some counterpoints. They may need translating. French: https://songazine.fr/v2/cbgb-temple-du-rock-underground-a-nyc/?utm_source=chatgpt.com Japanese: https://www.udiscovermusic.jp/stories/city-scenes-new-york-punk-cbgb?amp=1 Greek: https://www.lifo.gr/now/greece/cbgb-o-thrylos-toy-punk-sti-nea-yorki?utm_source=chatgpt.com

I can thank specific songs for help with two of the answers in today's crossword:

6A. CBGB is mentioned in "Life During Wartime" by Talking Heads: "This ain't no Mudd Club or CBGB / I ain't got time for that now"

(The next verse also mentions my hometown, Pittsburgh PA, where I first saw the Heads' outstanding concert film, Stop Making Sense.)

30A. ROUE is mentioned in "Sixteen Going on Seventeen" from the classic musical/film, "The Sound of Music": "Eager young lads and roués and cads / Will offer you food and wine"

Has anyone else had trouble with inaccuracies on the Statistics page?  I recently set a personal best for a Saturday and the Daily Solve Times did not update.  Same thing happened a year ago for a different day and the support people said it was impossible to fix.  Hard to believe literally no one in IT has a high enough permission to edit a record.
A cute 6 minutes. Thanks!!

CBGB crossing GMC on a Monday was one of the most Naticky Naticks in recent memory. The theme was… fine, I guess. But I just kept hitting too many speed bumps for it to feel like a typical breezy Monday.

It made me frown into my morning coffee, which is never a good thing on Monday.

Lucky guess at IONE/ROUE. Always nice to learn something new.

I’ve noticed a few of these trickier Mondays lately. Wonder if they’re trying to flatten the weekly difficulty curve. Mondays were beginning to feel like a test of how fast I could type on my phone, so I think I like the change (although that race was a fun transition from the end-of-week puzzles). Crosses like the above might put off new solvers, though.

Drat that SW corner. 

BUTTERFLY FLY
Jalapeño is misspelled. or is Neal is misspelled. Another swing & a miss by NYT.

In my never-ending quest to solve a puzzle in EXACTLY my average solve time on XWSTATS (without checking or sandbagging), I achieved my closest-ever result. "You solved this 0:01 (0.3%) faster than your Monday average." Sooooooo close!

Only tangentially related to the crossword today, but I thought I'd point out that some people erroneously pronounce habanero with ñ because of jalapeño.

This is called hyperforeignism. Some other examples you might hear are pronouncing cache as cashay, coup de grâce as coup de gras (grah), or chorizo as choritzo (like Italian, though it's Spanish in origin).

That was cute, but was fairly meaty for a Monday.

I'm guessing someone will now post stats going back to the beginning of time to argue that this was somehow the perfect Monday puzzle.

One more puzzle find. A Thursday from December 5, 2002 by Joe DiPietro. There was no reveal in that one, but, one note at the top that said "Missing (Q W Z). Anyway - some answers from top to bottom, with appropriate letters highlighted. arABCDrom epicdEFeat buGHIJinx caLMNOvice PoKeRSTUd So, Q W and Z were all missing. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=12/5/2002&h=34a ....

Not crazy about today’s puzzle.   Oddball clues and answers for a Monday.   But mainly I am noticing that the crosswords (all three Mini, Midi, and Main) are crashing repeatedly.    This is on an Apple iPad.   Hopefully Apple or NYT will fix soon.

Slowest Monday in years.  I just assumed 34D was NEiL and didn't catch the cross should've been RAMA.  Duh!!

⭐️⭐️⭐️

I know how much commenters here hate when you call clues bad.

This puzzle was full of bad clues.

Fun Monday. If my iPad hadn’t bugged out twice I think I would’ve beat my best Monday time - it felt that easy. Which only goes to show how differently folk’s experiences can be. 

Yesterday I just about gave up! I couldn’t get a foothold but finally finished it around midnight. Comments included those that would disagree about its difficulty.

So late in the day, I doubt anyone will see it but--
There are, in fact, EIGHT stars in the asterism--don't call it a "constellation"!--the Big Dipper! The second-last node on the "handle" of the Dipper is actually a "visible binary" system: Mizar, and the much fainter, but visible, Alcor. The pair are sometimes called "the Horse and Rider." In pre-Costco-optical-department societies, the ability to distinguish the two was used as an eye test.

(Actually, since Alcor is itself a binary system, and Mizar a quadruple one, there are a whopping six stars involved!)
Having a theme this cute on a Monday was a treat!

Oh what a sparkly, lovely, wee-bit crunchy-for-me Monday puzzle!
Thank you Ginny and Rajeswari for sharing your joy with us today!
I’m very likely getting dumber, but this past Tuesday was crazy hard for me and I didn’t finish this one. I miss gettable Monday and Tuesday puzzles.

My favorite encore clues from last week:

[Copy cats?]  (3)
[Moves a lot]  (4)


MEW
AWES

@Lewis

Just thought of a clever clue:

Someone who moves a lot? (7)

REALTOR

Jamie,
This is a themed Monday puzzle.
What's the "but" about?
@Barry Ancona I was sad that it was so quick, but I like a themed Monday puzzle like this one.

Jamie,
Thanks for the clarification!

@Morn
I liked the puzzle, but was one of the hardest Mondays for me in a while.

0
@Morn

This ain't no party, this ain't no disco
This ain't no fooling around
This ain't no Mudd Club, or C.B.G.B.
I ain't got time for that now

--Talking Heads "Life During Wartime"
@Morn Yeah I had a bit of trouble with IONE / ROUE
@Morn the "roue" and "Ione" intersect got me. Surprised so many have never heard of CBGB, though. I thought that was coming knowledge enough for a Monday but I guess not.

@Bob T.
Never saw them live.
Saw the film when it came out and then they released it for a big  anniversary, I forgot which number.
Rewatch certain parts alot.
Jonathan Demme directed the film.
One of my top concert films, the other being The Last Waltz.
Only went to CBGB once.  It was past it's heyday then and I mostly remember how sticky the floor was. 
Can't believe how apt the song is for these times.

Vaer,

SMEAR has appeared 227 times before, 
never once clued to cream cheese.

Last month, Lipstick blunder. 
Last year, Mascara mishap.
Daub, politics, etc., etc.
Never food, much less cream cheese.

@Vaer

Yes calling cream cheese on a bagel a SMEAR is a crime. Maybe cream cheese on an English muffin…

@SP

First of all, the clue said nothing about a bagel.  Second, I read smear as a verb, as in, "Smear this cream cheese on your toast.  It's delicious!"  To me, schmear is a noun, as in, "Do you want your bagel with a schmear?"

Doug,
If you put cream cheese on toast, you SPREAD it.

@Vaer

Happy birthday to Mel Brooks—although since his 2000 year old man bit started in 1950, that should put him at least at age 2076. I would link to one of those bits but there’s so many, just look them up if you want, nothing cracks me up more.

@Vaer
Apologies to Hedy.
Hedy Lamarr was an co-inventor of frequency hopping, helping to avoid eavesdropping on radio communiqués.

@Vaer
 That's Hedley. :)

@Dan
The clip with Mel that I posted was from an American Masters documentary on Hedy broadcast on Public Broadcasting Corporation stations.  When she's clued in the crossword, it's often with a reference to inventions or scientific endeavors .

@MC
That's gonna cost you.

@SP
I knew that album from camp. The counselors played it. There was a funny line about Joan of Arc. I've tried to post it here twice, but it doesn't seem to get through. You know the response...when the 2000-year-old man is asked:"You knew Joan of Arc??"

@lucky13
 It doesn’t translate very well in a transcript but here goes:

[REPORTER]
You knew Joan of Arc?

[2,000-YEAR-OLD MAN]
I went with her, dummy! I went with her.

[REPORTER]
Nowhere in history do we know of Joan going with anybody.

[2,000-YEAR-OLD MAN]
Well, they don't print that!

[REPORTER]
You didn't marry her.

[2,000-YEAR-OLD MAN]
No, no, I didn't marry her because she was on a mission. She used to say to me—She used to say to me "I gotta go save France." I used to say "Look, I gotta wash up. You save France. See ya later. After you save France, I'll wash up."

[REPORTER]
How did you feel about her being burned to the stake?

[2,000-YEAR-OLD MAN]
Terrible. Terrible. See I didn't- I didn't know.
@lucky13 No takers? The 2000-year-old man, of course, says: "I went with her, dummy." Here's the whole album, if you like really old slapstick comedy: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kKBt41kjddA
@Vaer Continuing OT -- I have to think there are a lot of readers here, I recommend Mel Brooks's autobiography "All About ME! My  Remarkable Life in Show Business!"

@Steve L
 
Re Sunday's puzzle, you referenced the two Norse gods. The third thing you were thinking of might have been Olav.

@Vaer

Yes, that was the one. Barry clued me in in the Sunday comments.

@Steve L
Missed that, obvi.

@Steve L

Oooh…
Meatballs and lingon.
Hope you had a few small potatoes on that plate?

My grandmother
 (my “mormor” which means mother’s mother)
was a saucy, spunky rule breaker.

Sometimes, even a law breaker.

She made her own lingon 
and filled two large tubs of it,
tried to hide them in her suitcase
and came to visit us.

Going through customs,
they opened her bag
and it looked like a bloodbath.

Leaked lingon looks like a murder scene.

Took extra-long to make it out of there.

Luckily, they didn’t speak Swedish or 
they’d have heard the curse words 
and name-calling.

She was a firecracker!

@CCNY

No, it was served with mashed potatoes.
https://www.cbgb.com/pages/cbgb-history

@SP

I wish FRANCON, which sounds like a cosplay thing where we all dress like how we think he looks and carry binoculars around, would figure out the ideal number of Hebrew words to include for each day of the week using barometric readings, sanscrit, higher mathematics, and the airspeed velocity of an unladen swallow.

@SP

The word for night in Hebrew is pronounced like the name Lila, not Leila. Jus’ sayin’.
Meant a telescope, not binoculars

@Steve L

Exactly, which is why I said the spelling was more in line with Arabic. But as a Hebrew speaker I was still able to figure it out because the root is similar.

@HeathieJ

Kind of. The Francis Conjecture is that for any two solvers A and B where A is typically one standard deviation above the mean in solving ability and B is one standard deviation below, there exists a Puzzle P such that B can solve it cleanly and A can’t. Did I summarize correctly, Francis?

@Cat Lady Margaret
Maybe it has been done already, but having regard to this particular community, I would be interested to hear language-based pet peeves from anyone out there who would care to offer them.  At or near the top of my list are:

1.  Mis-pronunciation of “nuclear” as if it had two “u”s and no “e”.  (I recall being surprised that George W. Bush was guilty on that count.)

2.  Mis-pronouncing “February” as if it had only one “r”.

3.  Using “different than” instead of “different from” — that the latter is correct is, IMHO, easily established by considering what follows the verb “differ” — and yet, the use of the former is so widespread, even among writers who should know better, I expect someone to argue that it has become correct through common usage.

@Mean Old Lady

In a nutshell:

BCBG stands for Country, Bluegrass, and Blues, which was the original intention of this music venue. Instead, it pivoted to becoming the launchpad for Punk and New Wave bands, making famous artists and bands previously unknown. 
 
The Cotton Club did this for swing and jazz.

The Apollo Theater did this for soul and R&B (Rhythm and blues.)

Studio 54, did this for disco music in the 1970's and pop in the 1980's.

The 3 of them were cultural focal points and had significant gravitational pull for musicians – on stage and in the audience – thus becoming legendary.
@Mean Old Lady https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CBGB

It doesn't require the tilde in English. https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/jalapeno (See the usage.) Abd be sure to have a tamale.

@AS

If you mean the tilde, the convention is that tildes are ignored for practical reasons, recognizing that this is not accurate linguistically and with apologies to Hispanic speakers. You may disagree, but the concession allows for more Hispanic words to be included in the puzzle which I think is a fair trade-off. Here is a quote from Will Shortz:

“First, it’s not just American crosswords that have this tradition. Crosswords in most countries follow the same rule.

“And second, if we had to include the tilde in NIÑO, for example, then NIÑO, basically, would never appear in our crosswords, because it would be so hard to cross it with another word on that letter. The same would be true for other words with diacritical marks rarely used in English.

“So for practical reasons as well as tradition, we ignore diacritical marks.”
But at least the editors finally had the good taste to start to clue ANO as Portuguese...

@Barry Ancona
i wondered who'd be the first to say tamale,  you or Steve. ;)

@AS

No, not really. It's just the norm,  leaving out anything that's not a letter of the American alphabet. 

Apostrophes, for example, are also left out.

And "açaí" (of which the accents establish the pronunciation ah-ssah-EE) becomes acai (which without the accents would be pronounced ah-kie, rhyming with "pie".) 

Notice that even names have their accents left out – Molière, Émile (Zola), Cézanne, Anaïs (Nin). 

It would be very limiting to not use anything that has a diacritic or punctuation mark. 

I think it's valid to point it out, just not as a NYT mistake but rather as a reminder of the correct spelling maybe for those who don't know. 
But, for the most part,  NYT crossnerds are good at knowing jalapeño from jalapeno, and Pelé from Pele (which, by the way, in Portuguese, without the accent, means "skin") ;-)

John,
